coredata.dc:title: Novel and stable CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I) nanocomposites for light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiaming
Chen, Zhaoping
Liu, Jianming
Mei, Enrou
Liang, Xiaojuan
Liu, Jianping
Xiang, Weidong
coredata.dc:description: CsPbX3 (X = Br, I) quantum dots (QDs) have gradually become the star materials in light-emitting diodes (LED), but its stability has a big impact on development. Here, a variety of novel CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites have been fabricated and the luminescence wavelength can be tunable by varying the halide nanocomposites (Br, I). What's particularly nice is that the original photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of CsPbBr1l2 QDs approaches 82% and it still has close to 74% after being combined with TS-1. Besides, the nanocomposites enhance stability resisting moisture, thermal stressing and light. In addition, a 460 nm InGaN blue chip, YAG:Ce3+ and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 were equipped with white light emitting diodes (WLEDs), with a luminous effect of 95 lm/W, a color rendering index of 80, and a color temperature of 5144 K. Meanwhile, wide color gamut (116% of the National Television Standards Committee, 86.3% of the ITU-R Recommendation BT. 2020) and great chromaticity coordinate (0.3425, 0.3408) are realized. This is a very important step to open up the awkward situation of the practical application of perovskite QDs materials in LED.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CsPbX3
TS-1
Photoluminescence quantum yield
Stability
WLEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of WLEDs with CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites
Fabrication of WLEDs with CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51872207 and 52072271).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the fabrication process, (b) XRD patterns and (c) Digital images of CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I).
(a) XPS full spectrum of the CsPbBr1I2-TS-1. XPS spectrum of (b) Cs 3d, (c) Pb 4f, (d) Br 3d, (e) I 3d, (f) Ti 2p, (g) Si 2p, (h) C 1 s and (i) O 1 s, respectively.
(a) TEM image of original TS-1. (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM, (d) SAED pattern and (e-j) EDX mapping images of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1.

(a) Pore size distribution of TS-1, which is calculated by Barret, Joyner and Halenda (BJH) methods. (b) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of pure TS-1 (black curve)and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposite(red curve).
(a) PL (solid lines) and absorption (dashed lines) spectra of the CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I) nanocomposites. (b) PL decay curves of the same nanocomposites.

Plots of relative PL intensity of colloidal CsPbBr1I2 and the CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 as a function (a) with the temperature from 20 °C to 120 °C, (b) under UV illumination time and (c) of the soaking time in water. (d) Digital photos of pure CsPbBr1I2 QDs and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 at different storage periods correspond to 0 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, and 10 h respectively.

Electroluminescent (EL) spectra of (a) blue chip, (b) YAG:Ce3+ phosphor and (c) CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposites powder, and insets including blue, green and red light, respectively. (d) The output spectrum of WLEDs consists of a 460 nm InGaN chip, YAG:Ce3+ phosphor, and a CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 (drive current of 20 mA). (e) The chromaticity coordinate and color gamut corresponding to WLEDs. The illustration is a picture of the WLEDs device.
Emission decay lifetimes of CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I) nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Novel and stable CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I) nanocomposites for light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Environmental Science, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life and Environmental Science, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CsPbX3 (X = Br, I) quantum dots (QDs) have gradually become the star materials in light-emitting diodes (LED), but its stability has a big impact on development. Here, a variety of novel CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites have been fabricated and the luminescence wavelength can be tunable by varying the halide nanocomposites (Br, I). What's particularly nice is that the original photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of CsPbBr1l2 QDs approaches 82% and it still has close to 74% after being combined with TS-1. Besides, the nanocomposites enhance stability resisting moisture, thermal stressing and light. In addition, a 460 nm InGaN blue chip, YAG:Ce3+ and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 were equipped with white light emitting diodes (WLEDs), with a luminous effect of 95 lm/W, a color rendering index of 80, and a color temperature of 5144 K. Meanwhile, wide color gamut (116% of the National Television Standards Committee, 86.3% of the ITU-R Recommendation BT. 2020) and great chromaticity coordinate (0.3425, 0.3408) are realized. This is a very important step to open up the awkward situation of the practical application of perovskite QDs materials in LED.

ce:section.ce:para: All-inorganic cesium lead halide perovskite quantum dots (QDs) [1] have attached plenty of attention because of excellent fluorescence properties [2–5] such as adjustable band gap, wide luminescence range, high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), low defect formation energy and good optical properties, etc., which enable them have certain advantages in solar cells [6,7], lasers [8,9], imaging [10], biotechnology [11–14] and photodetectors [15], particularly in light-emitting diodes (LED) [16–21]. Importantly, LED application not only pursues high efficiency and wide color gamut, but also the operation stability of devices, which is a great challenge to perovskite QDs [22]. Especially, narrow-band emitted red phosphors and CsPbX3 perovskite QDs have attracted wide attention in the field of LED [23–26]. Therefore, the biggest problem in the practical application of perovskite QDs is how to improve stability while maintaining excellent luminescence properties [27].

For the past few years, great efforts have been made to improve the stability of CsPbX3 and expend their application potential. Polymers [28], inorganic oxide [29–32], and glass [33] are widely used in encapsulating perovskite QDs. Besides, molecular sieves have gradually become hot materials to coat perovskite QDs. Molecular sieves are ordered mesoporous materials with uniform pore size, extremely high Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, large adsorption capacity, uniform mesoporous structure and other outstanding characteristics, which have great significance in improving the stability (thermal, water, light) of perovskite QDs and making it widely applied. Moreover, the resulting micrometer-sized nanocomposites after encapsulating the perovskite QDs would be directly compatible with mature packaging techniques for commercial WLEDs [34]. Sun [35] chose zeolite-Y molecular sieve to improve the stability of perovskite QDs under environmental conditions and operating conditions, and used them as phosphors for white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) with wide color gamut, up to 114% of the National Television Standards Committee (NTSC) standard. Li [36] embedded CsPbBr3 in SBA-15, MCM-41 and SAPO-34, and found that only SAPO-34 enhanced the hydrophobicity and dispersity. Li Liang's [37] research group successfully encapsulated CsPbBr3 QDs in dense SiO2 solid by taking advantage of the collapse characteristics of MCM-41 molecular sieve template at high temperature to make nanocomposites as stable as ceramics. Molecular sieves have gradually become important materials for the construction of high stability nanocomposites due to their inherent properties [38,39]. On the basis of the above theories, we further find that titanate molecular sieve (TS-1) can be a wonderful matrix for encapsulating CsPbX3 and applying in LED. Compared with the previous works, the PLQY of the nanocomposites are higher and the WLEDs prepared by this work have better properties.

In this work, TS-1 applied to perovskite QDs is proposed for the first time, which is a Ti-Si molecular sieve with a three-dimensional pore structure represented by ZSM-5, consisting of a Z-shaped channel and an oval straight path intersecting with it [40]. In the framework of TS-1, Ti at tetra-coordination position is the active center of selected oxidation reaction. In addition to maintaining the original topological structure of MFI molecular sieve, titanate forms the skeleton Si-O-Ti bond with special properties due to the uniform distribution of titanium atoms in the framework. The hollow structure isolates the inner cavity from the external environment through the ultra-thin shell, which has been widely used in the protection of metastable system [41]. This hollow structure ensures the stable growth of CsPbX3 in TS-1. Due to the comprehensive packaging of TS-1, CsPbX3 are locked in the TS-1 and separated from the environmental condition element, thus ensuring the environmental stability of perovskite QDs. More importantly, compared with the molecular sieves such as MCM-41, zeolite-Y, SBA-15 and others selected by people before, TS-1 is hydrophobic, which can greatly improve the water stability of perovskite QDs. Then we mixed CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 red powder produced in this experiment with YAG:Ce3+ phosphor powder and placed it on a 460 nm InGaN blue chip. The matched WLEDs show an excellent luminous efficiency of 95 lm/W and colour rendition index of 80, together with a color temperature of 5144 K. Meanwhile, wide color gamut (116% of the NTSC, 86.3% of the ITU-R Recommendation BT. 2020 (Rec. 2020)) and great chromaticity coordinate (0.3425, 0.3408) are realized.

Cesium carbonate (Cs2CO3, 99%, Acros organics), lead (Ⅱ) bromide (PbBr2, 99.0%, Aladdin), lead (Ⅱ) iodide (PbI2, 99.99%, Aladdin), oleic acid (OA, technical grade 90%, Aladdin), oleylamine (OAm, 80–90%, Aladdin), 1-octadecene (ODE, ＞90%, Aladdin), hexane (analytical reagent ≥ 97%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai)). TS-1 were purchased from Tianjin Yuanli Chemical Co., Ltd. Silicone resin A, and silicone resin B (Dow Corning, OE 6550), YAG:Ce3+, 460 nm InGaN LED chip. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

In a typical experiment, Cs2CO3 (0.814 g), OA (3 mL) and ODE (30 mL) were put into a 50 mL three-neck flask, heated and stirred at 120 ℃ for 1 h, and always kept in the atmosphere of nitrogen. The solution was then further heated to 150 ℃, and the reaction continues until the solution was clear and yellow (still in nitrogen), indicating that Cs2CO3 has reacted completely with OA. Cesium oleate precursors were stored under nitrogen and need to be preheated to 100 ℃ before further use, in order to prevent precipitation at room temperature.

2 mmol PbX2, 10 mL ODE, 3 mL OAm, 3 mL OA were added into a 50 mL three-neck flask and evacuated for 1 h at 120 ℃. Then, the PbX2 was heated to 150 ℃ in an atmosphere of nitrogen gas until it completely dissolved. Afterward, 0.5 mL of cesium oleate precursor preheated to 120 ℃ was injected into the prepared solution. After 10 s, the three-necked flask was placed in an ice bath and cooled to room temperature. The resulting compound was poured into centrifuge tubes and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitate obtained after the first centrifugation was dispersed in n-hexane or toluene of the same volume. After the second centrifugation at the speed of 8000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatant was taken.

10 mL of the perovskite QDs solution dispersed in n-hexane was selected, then the quantitative TS-1 was placed, and the mixed solution of perovskite QDs and titanate were stirred for 30 min. In order to make the perovskite QDs fully enter into TS-1 and then conduct ultrasonic treatment on the mixed solution, the air inside the TS-1 was discharged to make the perovskite QDs with appropriate particle size more fully penetrate into the mesoporous channel of TS-1, then centrifuged for 5 min at a speed of 8,000 rpm and dried for 10 h in an oven at 80 ℃ to obtain the final material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of WLEDs with CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The white LED device consists of YAG:Ce3+ phosphor, as-prepared CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 and 460 nm InGaN blue chip. First, we mixed silicone A and silicone B in equal amounts, then mixed with appropriate amount of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 and YAG:Ce3+ respectively and stirred well. Second, the homogeneous liquid mixture was then rotated and coated (3000 rpm, 30 s) onto the quartz plate and exposed to an UV lamp for a few minutes to form a white LED device.

X-Ray diffraction (XRD) was measured using Bruker D8 Advance measurements, wherein CuKα radiation was working environment at voltage 40 kV and current 40 mA in the boundary of 10° − 60° (2θ) at the velocity of 0.03° s-1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained by an spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images were measured using a FEI Tecnai G2F20S-TWIN transmission electron microscope with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, and EDS elements mapping images were were characterized to analysis elements of the samples. Pore size distribution of TS-1 is calculated by Barret, Joyner and Halenda (BJH) methods. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired by a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluromax-4P Spectrofluorometer, which employs a Xe lamp as an excitation source. The absorption spectra were obtained UV–vis Spectrometer of PerkinElmer Lambda 750. Time-resolved PL decay spectra was carried out by a spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, FLS920). The contact angle was measured using a Drop Shape Analyzer-DSA25. The thermal conductivity of TS-1 was measured by a thermal-detector (LFA427/4/G).

We make a flow chart to display the preparation process of CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I) in Fig. 1 a. Fig. 1b reveals the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the synthesized materials after combining CsPbX3 with TS-1. As can be seen from the Fig. 1b, the diffraction peaks of perovskite QDs are not obvious, the reason for this is the diffraction peaks of TS-1 are too strong. When perovskite QDs enter micron-sized molecular sieves, the diffraction signals are normally weak, which is consistent with the results of previously published papers [42]. In addition, Fig. 1c shows digital images of the CsPbX3-TS-1 composite materials under normal light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). The powder emits a bright fluorescence under UV light, indicating that the luminescent material has good application potential in WLEDs.

To further prove the presence of perovskite QDs in the nanocomposite, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed as shown in Fig. 2 . The elements of Cs, Pb, Br, I, Ti, Si, C and O elements are observed in the XPS full spectrum of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposite in Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b-i show the binding energies
of Cs 3d, Pb 4f, Br 3d, I 3d, Ti 2p, Si 2p, C 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. These above results indicate the coexistence of CsPbBr1I2 QDs and TS-1 matrix in the nanocomposite.

Based on the transmission electron microscope (TEM) of TS-1 (Fig. 3 a), the pores can be clearly observed in the image inserted in the upper right corner. In order to better explore the composite situation of perovskite QDs and TS-1, Fig. 3b displays the TEM images of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposite. According to the Fig. 3b, we can clearly see the embedding of perovskite QDs, a large number of nanoparticles are randomly distributed on the low-contrast matrix, indicating that a good composite result has been obtained between CsPbBr1I2 and TS-1. We also notice that the morphology of some perovskite QDs changes from cubic shape to spherical shape after being combined with TS-1 due to the different capping agents, which is a normal and reasonable phenomenon [43]. The pore size limiting effect of the TS-1 changes their profile into a spherical shape, implying the covering of TS-1 around the perovskite QDs core [44–46]. To further explore the morphology of nanocomposite, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 3c. The nanocomposite still shows clear lattice fringes and displays the lattice spacing of 2.74 Å, which located between the (012) face of the CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 cubic perovskite phases, indicating the high crystallinity of the CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 sample. Fig. 3d shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED). The measurement results show that the CsPbBr1I2 perovskite QDs have formed a complete crystal plane in the molecular sieve with a spacing of 1.78 Å and 2.44 Å, they are located between the (113) face and (112) face of the CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 cubic perovskite phases, respectively. The element mappings of Cs+, Pb2+, Br- and I- in CsPbBr1I2 are shown in Fig. 3e-h, while the element mappings of Si4+ and Ti3+ in molecular sieve are shown in Fig. 3i-j. It is not difficult to see that the crystal structure of the molecular sieve is mostly composed of Si element, Ti element are uniformly distributed in each crystal of the molecular sieve, and the Cs+, Pb2+, Br- and I- are mostly distributed in the pores of each molecular sieve crystal structure, and a small part is distributed in the interior. The cubic lattice of CsPbBr1I2 QDs are reavealed in Fig. S1a. Fig. S1b shows the HRTEM image of one piece of the grain in Fig. S1a. Fig. S1c reveals the XRD pattern of the bare CsPbX3 (X = Br, I) QDs. There are two preferential diffraction peaks along the (100) and (200) planes based on the standard card of cubic CsPbBr3 phase (ICSD-29073), respectively. The related diffraction peaks from CsPbBr3 to CsPbBr0.5I2.5 uniformly shift towards a lower angle, which is due to the substitution of the larger I ions [47]. The average size of CsPbBr1I2 QDs is 3.56 nm (Fig. S1d).

To accurately explore the pore size of TS-1, the porosity of this matrix is analyzed in Fig. 4 a. By BJH methods, desorption average pore width (4 V/A) is obtained (4.54 nm). Fig. 4b shows the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of pure TS-1 (black curve) and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposite (red curve). The mesoporous TS-1 show a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 363.3 m2/g, which is higher than those of other matrix reported in previous literatures. After introducing the CsPbBr1I2 QDs into TS-1, the BET surface area reduces to 140.1 m2/g. The great decrease of surface area proves that the CsPbBr1I2 QDs are successfully incorporated into TS-1.

In order to explore the influence of the ratio of perovskite QDs to TS-1 on its emission intensity, the PL spectra in Fig. S2 of the nanocomposites were measured at the ratios of 1: 3, 1: 5, 1: 10 and 1: 15 (CsPbBr1I2: TS-1), respectively. It is obvious that the CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 with a ratio of 1:5 exhibits the optimal emission intensity. Within a certain range, the PL intensity of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 will increase as the concentration of CsPbX3 increases. However, too high a concentration of CsPbX3 QDs will cause self-absorption, which leads to the decrease of PL intensity [48].

Fig. 5 a shows the emission (solid lines) and absorption (dashed lines) spectra of the CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites: CsPbBr3-TS-1 (green), CsPbBr2I1-TS-1 (olivine), CsPbBr1.5I1.5-TS-1 (yellow), CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 (red), CsPbBr0.5I2.5-TS-1 (dark red). The emission peaks are located at 517 nm, 540 nm, 580 nm, 639 nm and 665 nm, respectively. The full-widths-at-half-maximum (FWHM) of emission peaks are narrow, from top to bottom, corresponding to 12 nm, 14 nm, 18 nm, 20 nm and 21 nm, respectively. Fig. S3a shows the emission spectra of the perovskite QDs: CsPbBr3 (green), CsPbBr2I1 (olivine), CsPbBr1.5I1.5 (yellow), CsPbBr1I2 (red), CsPbBr0.5I2.5 (dark red). Besides, we note that with the increase in the I-ion concentration, the emission spectrum is wider [49], which similar to the colloidal QDs [50]. The PL decay curves of the CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 5b. As shown in the following function (1), a1 and a2 are corresponding weight factor for the τ1 and τ2, respectively. The obtained average PL decay lifetimes for these CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites were calculated by a weighted averaging equation (function (2)) [46]:
(1)
a 1
=
τ 1
τ 1
+
τ 2
a 2
=
τ 2
τ 1
+
τ 2
(2)
τ avg
=
a 1
τ 1 2
+
a 2
τ 2 2
a 1
τ 1
+
a 2
τ 2

Table 1 summarizes the optical properties of CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposite materials. The decay dynamics are faster for the lighter halide (Br > I), in agreement with previous results [51]. The τ lifetimes range from≈ 10.97 ns for CsPbBr3-based composite to≈ 53.92 ns for CsPbBr0.5I2.5-based composite, TS-1 maintains the fluorescence properties of QDs very well. In addition, Table S1 summarizes the PLQY of CsPbX3 (X = Br, I) QDs and CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I) nanocomposites.

The most difficult problem in the application of perovskite QDs is its instability under environmental condition element. Surprisingly, the environmental stability of perovskite QDs incorporated into TS-1 is greatly improved. Thermal stability of great importance for practical application in LEDs devices to ensure high efficiency [52]. Fig. S4a and d exhibit the PL spectra of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 and bare CsPbBr1I2 QDs solution at different heating temperature, respectively. To more directly compare the heating effect on fluorescence emission performance, normalized PL integrated intensity of the two samples as a function of different heating temperature were extracted and plotted in Fig. 6 a. As can be seen, when the temperature rises to 120 ℃, the PL intensity of the CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposites is 70% of its initial intensity, while the CsPbBr1I2 QDs have been completely quenched. In addition, the CsPbBr1I2 QDs show slight emission spectra red shift, and this is mainly because heating induces the falling off of surface ligands, which make more surface defects exposed and aggregation/regrowth of the bare perovskite QDs. In contrast, no detectable emission spectra shift of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 is observed. In addition, we tested the thermal conductivity of TS-1 (Table S2) and obtained the relevant value is 0.08657 W/mk. The smaller the value, the worse the thermal conductivity, and the better the insulation. This intuitively shows that TS-1 has obvious encapsulation effect on perovskite QDs, which greatly improves the thermal stability. Fig. 6b displays the changes of PL intensity as a function of UV illumination time of CsPbBr1I2 and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1, the test period is from 0 h to 90 h. After 90 h, the relative intensity of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 is decreased to nearly 74.9% while CsPbBr1I2 is almost zero. TS-1 can prevent photo oxidation during UV light irradiation, so that the light stability of CsPbBr3 QDs is greatly improved. The raw PL spectra can be found in Fig. S4b and e. In addition to the impact of the temperature and light, perovskite QDs are also easily decomposed into Cs+ and [PbX6]4- due to polar solvents. Water, widely exists in atmospheric environment, is also a polar solvent commonly used in chemical reagents [53]. This may be due to the ionic crystal properties of perovskite QDs, which will cause low formation energy and high ion migration activity once it meets water [54]. From Fig. 6c, there is little fluorescence intensity after 10 h when naked CsPbBr1I2 soaked in deionized water. Compared with bare perovskite QDs, the PL of CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 in aqueous solution dose not change significantly in the first 10 h, thus proving that TS-1 molecular sieving has an obvious protective effect on the packaging of perovskite QDs. The raw PL spectra can be found in Fig. S4c and f. Fig. 6d shows the digital photos of pure CsPbBr1I2 QDs and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 at different storage periods correspond to 0 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, and 10 h respectively. Although after a certain degree of oscillation, the colloidal CsPbBr1I2 shows a phase separation due to the insoluble nature of n-hexane and water [55] and most of the CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposites float on top due to the hydrophobic performance of the TS-1 [56]. TS-1 have excellent hydrophobicity with a water average contact angle of 103.1° (Fig. S5). In order to explore the level of stability of CsPbX3-TS-1 nanocomposites in our work, we make a comparison table with other composites as shown in Table S3 and find that the nanocomposites do have significantly stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of WLEDs with CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite QDs are suitable for application in LED due to the narrower PL spectra and superior PLQY compared to the traditional phosphors. We mix the CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 red powder produced in this experiment with YAG:Ce3+ fluorescent powder through AB glue, and place it on the 460 nm InGaN blue chip. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a-c, blue chip, YAG:Ce3+ phosphor and CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 nanocomposites powder can emit strong blue, olivine and red light, the insets are the photos of the generated light source. Fig. 7d shows the spectrum of a WLEDs made from CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 red powder, YAG:Ce3+ fluorescent and a blue chip at 20 mA. The luminous efficiency corresponding to the prepared WLEDs is 95 lm/W, the color rendering index is 80, and the relevant color temperature is 5144 K. At the same time, the chromaticity coordinate is (0.3425,0.3408), and the color gamut is presented in Fig. 7e (red line), which covers 116% of the NTSC color gamut (black line) and 86.3% of the Rec. 2020 color gamut (white line). Also, we equip with another set of data with CsPbBr3-TS-1, CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 and blue chip (Fig. S6), the luminous effect is 30.21 lm/W, the color rendering index is 93.1, and the relevant color temperature is 5299 K. For now, it is still difficult to achieve a light effect greater than 100 lm/W and a color rendering index greater than 90 at the same time. This is also the goal of our next efforts.

In summary, a series of novel CsPbX3-TS-1 (X = Br, I) have been fabricated for the first time. CsPbX3 (X = Br, I) are incorporated into TS-1 successfully, which have been proved by XRD, TEM, HRTEM, BJH and BET. The nanocomposites not only maintain the optical properties of perovskite QDs well, but also have excellent environmental stability. Materials with good stability, narrow emission range and high quantum efficiency are ideal active materials for many photoelectric application elements. Then we combine the YAG:Ce3+ phosphor, as-prepared CsPbBr1I2-TS-1 and 460 nm InGaN blue chip to form the WLEDs. The matched WLEDs show an excellent luminous efficiency of 95 lm/W and colour rendition index of 80, together with a color temperature of 5144 K. Meanwhile, wide color gamut (116% of the NTSC, 86.3% of the Rec. 2020) and great chromaticity coordinate (0.3425, 0.3408) are realized. Furthermore, this finding provides a facile and general strategy to modify and stabilize luminescent perovskite QDs and pave a critical way for application in WLEDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51872207 and 52072271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.131327.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Brightly Luminescent and Color-Tunable Colloidal CH3NH3PbX3 (X= Br, I, Cl) Quantum Dots: Potential Alternatives for Display Technology
